content: Just as linguistic reflection (the human cognitive and educational definition of reflexion) requires stepping outside one’s own immediate perspective to examine one’s writing or reasoning, LLM self-reflection mechanisms can borrow this structured human educational practice to build better multi-perspective reflection: instead of just generating contrasting solutions, LLM agents can be prompted to walk through the same structured reflection checklist high school students use to edit their own writing, to systematically audit their outputs for logical gaps and unstated assumptions. This turns ad-hoc LLM self-reflection into a teachable, generalizable skill.
